text: Active recovery clears accumulated blood lactate faster than passive recovery after high-intensity running.

Blood lactate clearance during active recovery is intensity-dependent, with higher intensities (60-100% of lactate threshold) clearing lactate more rapidly than lower intensities (0-40% of lactate threshold).
Maximum blood lactate clearance occurs at active recovery intensities close to the lactate threshold (80-100% of lactate threshold).

Active recovery at 80-100% of lactate threshold produces shorter time constants for 67% lactate clearance and higher peak clearance rates than recovery at 40% of lactate threshold or passive recovery.
High-intensity interval running at 90% VO2max increases blood lactate from approximately 1.0 to 3.9 mmol/L after 5-minute bouts.
Self-regulated higher-intensity active recovery enhances lactate clearance compared to self-regulated lower-intensity recovery, confirming the intensity dependence of clearance.
